question.text: Mr. Harish, a Resident and Ordinarily Resident (ROR) of India, earns interest income of Rs.8,00,000 from bonds in Country X. Country X deducts tax at 15% (Rs.1,20,000) at source. India's tax rate on this income slab is 20%. India has a DTAA with Country X following the Credit Method. What is Mr. Harish's net tax liability in India on this income?

answer.text: Nil, as tax is already paid in Country X

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Under the Credit Method, India (residence country) taxes the worldwide income at 20% = Rs.1,60,000, but gives credit for tax already paid in Country X (Rs.1,20,000). Net tax payable in India = Rs.1,60,000 - Rs.1,20,000 = Rs.40,000. **The Trap:** "Rs.1,60,000" ignores the credit entirely. "Nil" wrongly applies the Exemption Method. "Rs.1,20,000" confuses the credit amount with the liability. **Cross-Exam Context:** NISM Series V-A (Mutual Fund) and NISM Currency Derivatives exams test basic tax concepts for NRIs. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Always compute the differential tax — the credit method means the investor pays the higher of the two countries' rates, not both cumulatively.
